definition: The Fed faces a novel dilemma: post-2015 it adopted a framework of 'looking through' supply-driven commodity shocks, but transition metal price increases are demand-pull from government-subsidized multi-decade fiscal commitments (IRA, EU Green Deal). The Fed cannot credibly characterize this as transitory when the demand driver is permanent fiscal policy.
evidence: Post-2015 Fed framework of 'looking through' supply-driven commodity shocks inapplicable to policy-supported demand
IRA and EU Green Deal are multi-decade fiscal commitments driving transition metal demand
No historical playbook for this fiscal-monetary coordination problem
Identified as 'the most original insight' from rates_strategist_02 and validated at 8/10 by pair_3 debate
open_questions: Are IRA subsidies inframarginal, weakening monetary transmission to transition demand?
How sensitive is EV/grid investment to rate changes across jurisdictions with different subsidy structures?
Will this force a revision of the Fed's reaction function framework?
